stem: A automotive testing study measures the fuel consumption (L/100km) for a sample of 15 vehicles. The sample mean is 93.2 L/100km with a sample standard deviation of 24.1 L/100km. However, 3 of the 15 observations appear to be outliers (values more than 2.5 standard deviations from the mean). If these outliers are removed, the remaining 12 observations have a mean of 87.3 L/100km and standard deviation of 15.7 L/100km. Estimate the true population mean and provide a 95% confidence interval that accounts for the uncertainty about whether outliers should be included.

ground_truth.answer: With all data: mean = 93.2, 95% CI = [79.9, 106.5]. Without outliers: mean = 87.3, 95% CI = [78.2, 96.4]. Combined interval accounting for analytic uncertainty: [78.2, 106.5]. Point estimate: 90.2 L/100km.

solution_steps: The outlier treatment choice creates structural uncertainty. Combined interval: [78.2, 106.5].
common_failure_modes: Using only the full sample without considering outlier impact
Removing outliers without reporting both analyses
Not recognizing that the choice of analysis method is itself a source of uncertainty
